topics: 📐 Estimating field damage — bet kor too big, bet kav too small
🍇 R' Akiva's refinements: ripe vs. unripe, trees vs. produce
🌾 Mishnah 6:3 — stacking sheaves with vs. without permission
🔥 Mishnah 6:4 — sending fire through a deaf-mute / minor
💨 Wind as the fire-spreader — pikadon vs. force majeure

overview.title: 📖 Overview — Damage Math, Sheaves, and Fire
overview.label: The sugya:
overview.text: The daf finishes the grazing-damages math (why you don't measure by an unfair-large or unfair-small parcel), surveys R' Akiva's adjustments for unripe vs. mature produce, and pivots to Mishnah 6:3's neat reversal: stack sheaves without permission and the field-owner's animal eats them — you swallow the loss; stack with permission and the owner has to pay if his animal damages them. Then comes perek 6:4 — fire. The Mishnah builds a chain of liability through deaf-mutes, agents, woodcarriers, and finally the wind itself.
overview.hebrewSource: אֵין שָׁמִין בֵּית כּוֹר מִפְּנֵי שֶׁהוּא פוֹגֵם. וְלֹא בֵית קַב מִפְּנֵי שֶׁמַּשְׁבִּיחַ. אֶלָּא שָׁמִין בֵּית סְאָה

slides.title: 📐 Why a Bet Se'ah Is the Right Yardstick
slides.subtitle: The Tanna's middle path between fairness extremes.
rows: Too big — averages out the damage, victim under-compensated
Too small — local damage looks worse than it is
Right balance — represents the surrounding field fairly

callout.label: Yannai variations:
callout.text: Beit Yannai: שָׁמִין תַּרְקָב בְּשִׁשִׁים תַּרְקַבִּים — three-kav unit in 60 of them. R' Yose ben Chanina: קֶלַח בְּשִׁשִּׁים קְלָחִים — even one stalk in 60. Different scales of the same averaging principle.

slides.title: 🍇 R' Akiva's Tiers — Pricing the Damage
slides.subtitle: Different produce stages get different valuations.

boxes.title: 🌱 Shoots / לולבי גפנים
items: Ate fig shoots or vine shoots
R' Yehudah: this is the bet-se'ah case

boxes.title: 🍇 Unripe / בוסר ופגין
items: Loss is the harvest the owner would have had

boxes.title: 🌳 Mature trees / נטיעות
items: R' Yehudah b/n R' Akiva: damage by saplings
Trees valued by tree, not by produce

callout.label: Sharper rule:
callout.text: R' Shimon ben Yehudah: תְּבוּאָה שֶׁלֹּא הֵבִיאָה שְׁלִישׁ נִידּוֹנֶת כִּנְטִיעוֹת — grain that hasn't reached one-third growth is treated like saplings, not produce.

slides.title: 🌾 The Sheaves Mishnah — Permission Reverses Liability
slides.subtitle: Same physical situation, opposite legal results.

nodes.content: You stacked sheaves on someone else's field

columns.label: Without permission
columns.content: Field-owner's animal ate them → he is פטור. Your sheaves were the trespass.

columns.label: With permission
columns.content: Field-owner's animal ate them → he is חייב. He invited the sheaves; he owes shemira.

nodes.content: R' Yitzchak's question: Are sheaves on a field treated like vessels in a house?
R' Yose b. R' Bun: only when the field אִית לֵיהּ מִסְגָּר — has a closure/fence

callout.label: Symbolic pivot:
callout.text: Permission isn't a polite formality — it's the moment legal responsibility shifts. Without it you're a trespasser; with it the owner becomes the shomer.

slides.title: 🔥 The Fire Mishnah — Chain of Causation
slides.subtitle: Each link in the chain shifts liability to whoever made it dangerous.

steps.content: 1️⃣ Sent through a חרש שוטה וקטן → exempt בדיני אדם, חייב בדיני שמים.
2️⃣ Sent through a competent person → that person is liable.
3️⃣ One brought the fire, another the wood → whoever brought the wood is liable.
4️⃣ One brought wood, another the fire → whoever brought the fire is liable.
5️⃣ A third person fanned the flames → that person is liable. The wind fanned it → all are exempt.

callout.label: The principle:
callout.text: Liability follows the active completing cause — whoever turns potential into damage takes the loss. Once nature (the wind) takes over, the chain breaks.

slides.title: 💨 What Kind of Wind Breaks the Chain?
slides.subtitle: Catastrophic vs. ordinary, and the implications.

lines.speaker: Tamman
lines.text: The Mishnah's exemption is for רוּחַ שֶׁלְּאוֹנְסִין — catastrophic wind. Normal wind keeps you liable.

lines.speaker: R' Y / RL
lines.text: Even normal wind: אֲפִילוּ רוּחַ שֶׁהָעוֹלָם מִתְנָהֵג בּוֹ פָּטוּר. Why? שֶׁפְּעָמִים בָּא פְּעָמִים לֹא בָא — wind isn't predictable enough to count as your direct extension.

lines.speaker: Chizkiyah
lines.text: Sub-debate: Did you hand him a גחלת (coal) or a שלהבת (flame)? A coal can fizzle out — flame is unstoppable.

lines.speaker: R' Yochanan
lines.text: הִיא גַחֶלֶת הִיא שַׁלְהֶבֶת — same thing.

callout.label: What's at stake:
callout.text: How predictable does the spread mechanism need to be for it to count as your fire? The stricter view (Tamman) treats normal weather as your tool. The lenient view treats unpredictability as a break in the chain.

summary.principle: Damage valuation needs the right yardstick (bet se'ah, not too big or too small). Liability follows the active cause through chains of agency — and breaks when nature (wind) or unpredictability takes over.

flowSteps.content: How do we measure damage, and where does liability stop?

🎓 Key Principle:
Halakhic damage tracks fair measurement and active causation — never average abstraction or passive proximity.

quiz.q: Why doesn't the Mishnah measure damage by a bet kor or bet kav?
o: Bet kor is too big (פוגם), bet kav too small (משביח) — bet se'ah balances
Because of a takanah of Beit Hillel
Only fields are measured, not orchards
quiz.e: אֵין שָׁמִין בֵּית כּוֹר מִפְּנֵי שֶׁהוּא פוֹגֵם וְלֹא בֵית קַב מִפְּנֵי שֶׁמַּשְׁבִּיחַ — bet se'ah is the fair midpoint.

quiz.q: When is the field-owner liable for his animal eating sheaves?
quiz.e: Permission flips liability: with permission, the owner becomes the shomer and pays; without permission, the stacker is the trespasser.

quiz.q: What is the rule when fire is sent via a חרש, שוטה, or קטן?
o: Exempt בדיני אדם, חייב בדיני שמים
quiz.e: Beit din can't compel, but heaven holds the sender accountable for using an irresponsible agent.

quiz.q: When the wind fans the flames, what does the Mishnah say?
o: הֲרֵי כוּלָּן פְּטוּרִין — all are exempt
quiz.e: Once the wind takes over, the chain of human causation breaks — even according to R' Yochanan and Reish Lakish, regardless of how routine the wind.

quiz.q: What is R' Yochanan and Reish Lakish's reasoning to exempt even ordinary wind?
o: פְּעָמִים בָּא פְּעָמִים לֹא בָא — wind is unpredictable
Only fire ignited by a person counts
quiz.e: Unpredictability breaks the causal chain even with everyday wind, since you can't reliably anticipate it as your tool.
